knowledge.statement: Continuous Integration (CI) automatically builds and tests code on every commit. The goal is to detect integration issues early, when they are cheap to fix, rather than at the end of a long development cycle.

Continuous Delivery (CD) ensures the codebase is always in a releasable state and can be deployed at any time with a manual trigger. Continuous Deployment goes further and releases every passing commit automatically.

A CI pipeline should run in under 10 minutes. Pipelines that take longer discourage frequent commits and reduce the feedback loop value. Parallelise stages, cache dependencies, and defer slow tests to a separate stage.
Build artifacts should be built once and promoted through environments (dev → staging → production). Rebuilding the same commit per environment risks environment-specific binary differences.

Blue/Green deployment runs two identical environments. Traffic switches from the current environment (Blue) to the new one (Green) once it passes health checks. Rollback is instant: switch traffic back to Blue.

Canary deployment gradually routes a small percentage of traffic to the new version before full rollout. Metrics are monitored during the canary window; issues trigger an automatic rollback before most users are affected.
Rolling deployment replaces instances one at a time with the new version, keeping the system running throughout. Both old and new versions coexist briefly, so they must be API-compatible.
Feature flags decouple deployment from release. Code ships to production behind a flag and is turned on independently, enabling instant rollback without redeployment and supporting A/B experiments.
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) defines servers, networks, and databases in version-controlled files that are applied programmatically. Terraform, Pulumi, and AWS CDK are popular tools.
Immutable infrastructure means servers are never modified after deployment. Changes are made by building a new server image and replacing old instances, eliminating configuration drift.

Configuration drift occurs when servers in the same environment diverge over time due to manual changes. IaC and immutable infrastructure prevent drift by making declared state the only path to change.
Docker packages an application and its dependencies into a container image. Containers run identically across environments. A Dockerfile defines each step of the image build.
Docker multi-stage builds use multiple FROM statements to produce a small final image. Build-time tools (compilers, test runners) are discarded, reducing image size and attack surface.
Container images should run as a non-root user, use a minimal base image (distroless or alpine), and exclude development tools. Each constraint reduces the blast radius of a container compromise.

Kubernetes (K8s) automates deployment, scaling, and management of containerised applications. Core concepts: Pod (smallest deployable unit), Deployment (manages ReplicaSets), Service (stable network endpoint), Namespace (isolation boundary).

Kubernetes liveness probes restart a container if it becomes unresponsive. Readiness probes remove a pod from Service endpoints if it cannot yet handle traffic. Both are required for zero-downtime deployments.
Resource requests and limits must be set on every Kubernetes pod. Requests guide the scheduler; limits prevent one pod from starving its neighbours and causing cascading evictions.
Helm is the Kubernetes package manager. Charts bundle manifests with configurable values, enabling repeatable deployment of complex multi-resource applications across clusters and environments.
GitOps uses Git as the single source of truth for infrastructure and application state. Automated reconciliation agents (ArgoCD, Flux) continuously sync the cluster to the desired state in Git.

Zero-downtime deployment requires that the new version can process all request types from the old version. This demands backwards-compatible schema changes, versioned APIs, and graceful connection draining.
Database migrations must be backwards compatible across deployments. Add columns before they are used; delete them after code no longer references them. Never rename a column in a single deployment.

The expand-contract (parallel change) pattern for zero-downtime migrations has three phases: expand (add new schema), migrate (backfill data, update code), and contract (remove old schema). Each phase is a separate deployment.

Graceful shutdown allows a process to finish in-flight requests before stopping. Kubernetes sends SIGTERM before SIGKILL; applications must catch SIGTERM, stop accepting new connections, and drain existing ones.
Dev/prod parity means development and production environments should be as similar as possible in configuration, dependencies, and data volume. Differences cause bugs that only surface in production.
A staging environment should receive production-like traffic and data to catch performance regressions before they reach users. A staging environment with no realistic load gives false confidence.
Rollback must be planned before deployment, not after an incident. Verify that the previous version can be deployed quickly and that database migrations have a tested rollback path.
Docker images in production must use a specific version tag (e.g. v1.2.3), never 'latest'. The 'latest' tag is mutable, making deployments non-reproducible and rollbacks unreliable.
Build caching speeds CI by reusing unchanged Docker layers and dependency caches. Cache keys should hash lock files (package-lock.json, requirements.txt) to invalidate when dependencies change.
Trunk-based development has all engineers commit to a single main branch frequently. Long-lived feature branches are replaced by feature flags. It is the prerequisite for continuous deployment.
Branch protection rules enforce code review, passing CI, and up-to-date branches before merging. They prevent accidental direct pushes to the main branch and ensure quality gates cannot be bypassed.

Container registries store and distribute Docker images. Images should be scanned for vulnerabilities (Trivy, Snyk) before push and before pull for deployment. Use private registries for proprietary code.
The four DORA metrics are deployment frequency, lead time for changes, change failure rate, and mean time to restore (MTTR). Elite performers deploy multiple times per day with sub-hour lead times.

Kubernetes ConfigMaps hold non-sensitive configuration; Secrets hold sensitive values. Secrets should be stored encrypted at rest (KMS) and sourced from an external secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) at runtime.

Load balancer health checks remove unhealthy instances from the pool before they affect users. The health endpoint should exercise the application's critical dependencies, not just return a static 200 OK.
Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) in Kubernetes scales pod count based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics. Autoscaling requires stateless services; session affinity prevents proper scale-in.
Deployment gates automatically validate releases against error rate, latency, and business metric thresholds and halt or roll back if criteria are not met, preventing bad releases from propagating.
Terraform remote state must be stored in a shared backend (S3 + DynamoDB, Terraform Cloud) and locked during apply. Local state is single-user only and is lost when the local machine is replaced.
CI/CD service accounts should follow least privilege: only the minimum cloud IAM permissions required to deploy. Avoid long-lived admin credentials in environment variables; prefer OIDC federation.
Dependency pinning (lock files) ensures reproducible builds. Floating version ranges mean a build today may install a different package version than yesterday, making failures non-deterministic.

A pre-deployment checklist covers migration readiness, rollback plan, feature flag states, monitoring in place, and stakeholder communication. Checklists prevent the category of failures caused by omission under pressure.

Serverless functions (Lambda, Cloud Functions) scale to zero and remove server management. Key trade-offs: cold starts add latency, execution duration is limited, and distributed tracing requires explicit instrumentation.

Container-as-a-service platforms (Cloud Run, App Runner, Fly.io) combine container portability with serverless operational simplicity. They are a good default when full Kubernetes control-plane overhead is not justified.
Monitor error rate, latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99), and key business KPIs for at least 30 minutes after every production deployment before declaring it successful.

Idempotent deployments can be run multiple times without side effects beyond the first. Migrations, seed scripts, and provisioning steps should be idempotent to support safe reruns after partial failures.

Secrets must never be stored in Docker images, version control, or CI logs. Use a secrets manager (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) and inject secrets at runtime as environment variables or mounted files.
Dynamic configuration allows application behaviour to change at runtime without redeployment. Feature flags, config services (Consul, AppConfig), and environment variables are the main mechanisms.

Pod Disruption Budgets (PDB) in Kubernetes guarantee a minimum number of available pods during voluntary disruptions (node drains, cluster upgrades). Without a PDB, all pods of a deployment can be evicted simultaneously.
Network policies in Kubernetes restrict traffic between pods by default-deny rules. Without policies, all pods in a cluster can communicate with all others, violating least-privilege networking.
Artifact versioning with semantic versions tied to Git commit SHAs creates an unambiguous audit trail from a running service back to the exact source code and CI run that produced it.

Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) handles mutual TLS, traffic shifting, retries, circuit breaking, and observability at the infrastructure layer without code changes, enabling consistent cross-service policies.
Smoke tests run immediately after deployment to validate that critical paths are functional in the new environment. They give fast go/no-go signal before canary ramp-up or final cutover.

Progressive delivery combines feature flags, canary releases, and automated metric-based rollbacks into a single deployment strategy. It separates who sees a feature from whether it is deployed to production.

Cost tagging in cloud environments associates every resource with the team, service, and environment that owns it. Without tags, cost attribution is guesswork and over-provisioned dev resources go unnoticed.

Chaos engineering deliberately injects failures (pod kills, network partitions, CPU stress) in a controlled environment to verify that the system tolerates them gracefully. Resilience must be proven, not assumed.
The deployment pipeline is production code and should be treated as such: version controlled, reviewed, tested, and documented. Undocumented one-off pipeline scripts are a common source of outages.

Kubernetes init containers run to completion before the main container starts. They are used to wait for dependencies (database readiness), pre-populate caches, or run migrations before the application starts.
Supply chain security (SLSA levels) verifies that build artifacts come from a known, unmodified source. Signed builds, reproducible builds, and SBOM generation are key supply chain controls.
Warm-up time matters for JVM and other runtime-compiled languages: canary instances should receive warm-up traffic before performance metrics are compared against the baseline.

Deployment frequency is a leading indicator of team health. Low frequency typically indicates large batch sizes, long cycle times, and fear of deployment — not caution. The fix is smaller changes, not slower cadence.
